skill.name: adaptyv
skill.description: Cloud laboratory platform for automated protein testing and validation; use when you have designed protein sequences and need wet-lab experimental validation (e.g., binding, expression, thermostability, enzyme activity) and API-based submission/status/result retrieval.
skill.summary: Bridge computational protein design with wet-lab experimental validation through the Adaptyv cloud lab API.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation and reference skill for the Adaptyv cloud laboratory platform. Static analysis flagged 354 potential issues, but the overwhelming majority occur within markdown code examples and API documentation, not executable code. The only executable script (scripts/validate_skill.py) is a trivial argparse helper. Confirmed true positive risk factors include env_access (API key configuration for the Adaptyv service), network (API calls to Adaptyv AWS Lambda endpoint), external_commands (shell commands in documentation), and filesystem (result file writes in examples). The critical heuristic finding for a dangerous pattern combination is a false positive as all patterns reside in documentation context, not active code. Risk level is medium due to required API credentials and external network dependency.

medium_findings.title: API Key Configuration via Environment Variable
medium_findings.description: The skill documents storing ADAPTYV_API_KEY in environment variables or .env files for authentication with the Adaptyv cloud lab API. This is standard practice for API-based skills but requires users to handle credentials securely. The environment variable approach is appropriate, and the documentation correctly warns against committing keys to version control.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed pattern of env_access for API key storage. Pattern is legitimate cloud lab API usage, not credential exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Network Requests to External AWS Lambda API Endpoint
medium_findings.description: Code examples throughout the skill documentation make HTTP requests to an external Adaptyv API endpoint (AWS Lambda URL). Network calls are the core functionality of this skill for submitting experiments and retrieving results. The endpoint URL is hardcoded in multiple documentation files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed API endpoint URL in documentation. Pattern matches documented Adaptyv Bio cloud lab service - legitimate use case.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Examples in Documentation
medium_findings.description: Markdown documentation contains extensive shell command examples using backtick syntax for pip install, Python execution, and environment setup. These are documentation patterns showing users how to run commands, not active code execution. No command injection vectors or dynamic shell construction was found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern confirmed but limited to documentation code blocks. Shell commands are illustrative references, not dynamically executed. Low risk for AI execution context.

medium_findings.title: File Write Operations in Code Examples
medium_findings.description: Python code examples in documentation include file write operations (json.dump, open().write) for saving experiment results. These are legitimate output operations in a documentation context showing users how to persist results locally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed filesystem write operations, but all occur in documentation code blocks as examples for users. No active filesystem modification by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detected in Documentation Context
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged various 'weak cryptographic algorithm' patterns across reference markdown files and the adaptyv_audit_result_v2.json file. In all cases, these are false positives: the reference files contain API documentation with data format examples and amino acid sequence specifications, not cryptographic operations. The audit result JSON contains Base64-encoded API response examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear false positive - flagged patterns are amino acid codes, API schema examples, and help text strings, not encryption algorithms.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Sequence in FASTA Example Data
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'path traversal sequence' at reference/api_reference.md:40. The flagged text '...' is part of a FASTA sequence example showing truncated amino acid sequences, not a file path traversal pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Definitive false positive - ellipsis in biological sequence example, completely unrelated to file system path traversal.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Patterns in Documentation
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged multiple 'system reconnaissance' and 'network reconnaissance' patterns in documentation. These are false positives - the flagged lines contain descriptive text, experiment status checks, and data parsing code examples, not system information gathering.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear false positive - flagged patterns are print statements and status checks in example code, not reconnaissance activities.

low_findings.title: AWS Credentials Directory Reference in Documentation
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged reference to AWS credentials at protein_optimization.md:600 as 'sensitive: AWS credentials directory'. The referenced line contains a URL string in API documentation context, not actual AWS credential access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: False positive - the flagged item is a documentation reference URL, not an active AWS credential file read operation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Heuristic: Suspicious Pattern Combination in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged a heuristic pattern for 'DANGEROUS COMBINATION: Code execution + Network + Credential access' and 'SUSPICIOUS COMBINATION: Filesystem + Credentials + Network'. These heuristics are triggered because the documentation simultaneously contains API key examples, network request code, and file write operations. However, all patterns reside in separate markdown code blocks as illustrative documentation for the Adaptyv Bio API. The only executable script (scripts/validate_skill.py) is a trivial argparse helper that only prints 'validation-helper: ok'. No actual code execution path combines all three risk factors.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Heuristic triggered by co-occurrence of API key, network calls, and file writes in documentation. However, all patterns are in separate documentation code blocks, no combined execution path exists.

content.user_title: Run automated protein testing and validation via cloud lab API
content.value_statement: Design protein sequences but need wet-lab experimental validation? This skill connects your computational protein designs to the Adaptyv cloud laboratory platform for automated experimental testing. Submit sequences via API, track experiment status, and retrieve binding, expression, thermostability, or enzyme activity results.
actual_capabilities: Submit protein sequences for wet-lab experimental validation via the Adaptyv API
Track experiment status and progress through completion
Retrieve binding assay results including KD, kon, and koff measurements
Obtain expression testing results with yield and solubility data
Access thermostability measurements including melting temperature
Download enzyme activity results with kinetic parameters
limitations: Results take approximately 21 days from submission to delivery
Requires a valid Adaptyv Bio API key and active credits
Limited to Adaptyv supported assay types only
API is in alpha/beta and endpoints may change without notice

use_cases.title: Validate computationally designed protein variants
use_cases.description: After using AI or computational tools to design new protein sequences, use this skill to submit them for wet-lab binding or expression testing to confirm they perform as expected.

use_cases.title: Run high-throughput antibody screening campaigns
use_cases.description: Submit batches of antibody variants for binding affinity measurement against target antigens. Use results to select top candidates for further development.

use_cases.title: Integrate experimental validation into automated ML pipelines
use_cases.description: Feed experimental results back into protein design ML models to close the design-build-test cycle. Use API-based submission and webhook callbacks for programmatic workflow integration.
use_cases.target_user: ML scientists working on protein design

prompt_templates.title: Submit a binding assay experiment
prompt_templates.prompt: I have designed a protein sequence and need to test its binding affinity to a target. Use the Adaptyv skill to submit a binding assay experiment with my sequence.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with a single protein submission

prompt_templates.title: Compare multiple protein variants
prompt_templates.prompt: I have designed several variants of my lead protein and want to compare their expression levels. Use the Adaptyv skill to submit a batch expression testing experiment for all variants.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize sequence before wet-lab testing
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a set of candidate protein sequences. First use the protein optimization workflow to check for issues like unpaired cysteines and low solubility, then submit the top candidates for expression testing.

prompt_templates.title: Set up automated experiment tracking
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to submit experiments to Adaptyv and receive results via webhook when they complete. Use the skill to configure experiment submission with a webhook callback URL and set up status monitoring.

output_examples.input: Submit a protein sequence for binding assay testing against human PD-L1 target
output_examples.output: The system submitted the experiment and returned: Experiment ID exp_abc123xyz with status 'submitted' and estimated completion in 21 days. The results will include KD, kon, and koff values measured via BLI.

output_examples.input: Check the status of experiment exp_abc123xyz and download results if ready
output_examples.output: Experiment exp_abc123xyz status: 'completed'. Results downloaded successfully: 4 sequences tested. Top binder shows KD of 2.5 nM (strong affinity). Results saved with confidence rating 'high' and R-squared of 0.98.

best_practices: Use webhook callbacks instead of polling for production workflows to avoid unnecessary API requests
Pre-screen sequences computationally before submission to reduce experimental costs and focus on promising candidates
Include wild-type or reference sequences as controls in every batch submission for meaningful comparison
anti_patterns: Do not submit sequences without first checking for unpaired cysteines, excessive hydrophobicity, or other expression issues
Do not poll for status more frequently than every 10 seconds to avoid rate limiting and unnecessary API usage
Do not hardcode API keys in scripts or documentation; use environment variables or secure secret management

faq.question: What types of experiments does Adaptyv support?
faq.answer: Adaptyv supports binding assays (BLI), expression testing, thermostability measurements, and enzyme activity assays.

faq.question: How long does it take to get results?
faq.answer: Standard turnaround is approximately 21 days from submission to results delivery.

faq.question: Do I need my own API key to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. You must request API access from support@adaptyvbio.com and set your ADAPTYV_API_KEY as an environment variable.

faq.question: Can I submit multiple sequences at once?
faq.answer: Yes. You can submit batch experiments with multiple sequences in FASTA format. Batch submissions are encouraged for large-scale testing.

faq.question: How do I get notified when an experiment completes?
faq.answer: Configure a webhook URL during experiment submission to receive automatic notifications when results are ready.

faq.question: What format should my protein sequences be in?
faq.answer: Sequences should be provided in FASTA format with standard single-letter amino acid codes.
